title: What an American thinks about Malasia

transcript.text: Okay. What an American thinks about Malaysia. Malaysia. Oh, my. Malaysia. Malaysia was a surprising country for me. I thought Malaysia was just another one of these Southeast Asian third world countries like Thailand.

Um, you know, like dirt everywhere and poor people trying to rip you off and, um, um, you know, trash everywhere and people not speaking English and people being grumpy with you. It was kind of, I experienced some of that in Thailand, but I go to Malaysia and the infrastructure is way better.

They've got like paved roads, big, wide roads. They've got like convenience stores, like American style convenience stores everywhere. Whereas in Thailand, it's just like these little roadside stalls. It's kind of like Thailand would be Mexico and Malaysia would be like America. And they could all speak fluent English in Malaysia. It was crazy. They could all speak fluent English in Malaysia.

Um, but the weird thing about that was that you go to the movie theater and all the movies are Chinese and everywhere else in the world, even where they don't speak English at all, you go to the movie theater and all the movies are American. Like in Korea, they don't speak English at all, but all the movies are American.

I think, uh, same thing, same thing, same thing. You go to Malaysia and it's all Chinese movies in China. Malaysia is like a big mix. It's like Chinese people, native Malaysians who are like Indonesians, you know, and, and then you got the Indians who come over and, um, and Kuala Lumpur looked pretty. And there was also like lots of Muslims there too. Like they had a mosque in Kuala Lumpur and Kuala Lumpur seemed really like, uh, you know, once you get in the center of it, like,

A normal first world city, you know, they still had more like street stalls and stuff than your average, like first world city. But still, it seemed like good, uh, um, infrastructure and the people seemed friendly too. They were like smiling at me and stuff. I went to, let's see, Georgetown, Malaysia, which is part of Malaysia and this island. And, uh, yeah, people were cool. They have a really good, um, uh, health system there too. So, so social health system.

Um, cause I was having like asthma problems and I had to keep going to the, the doctor, the hospital, um, to get blood tests. I thought I was like dying of asthma and I got really sick one day at one time actually. And I went to, yeah, I actually went to a hospital in a bunch of different places and they would like give me, they'd give me free treatment, even though I'm not even a Malaysian.
It's cause I'm a person. They'd give me free treatment, free meeting with the doctor and then free, um, inhaler. I mean, that's way better than America. America's crap for that.

Like if you want to buy it, you can't go by. And then you can buy an inhaler at the store. They would like give me free ones just cause I go there. And, uh, yeah, it's wild. Um, you know, like you're in America, you can't buy any of that in the store. You're fucked. You have to go to the doctor, pay, wait for an hour, drive all the way across town, wait for an hour, pay them 150 bucks.

And then they'll give you a prescription for like three of them. Fucking travesty. But anyways, yeah. So they had a really good system there. So Malaysia, you know, it's pretty impressive. Um, but I heard they have like really strict, like drug laws, like they'll cut your head off or whatever. If you smoke weed or something, but I don't know. Um, so that's that.
